title: Multi-die Testing In The Field Must Build On Established Test Methodologies
gist: In-system (in-field) testing is becoming essential for multi-die 2.5D/3D-IC systems in data centers and automotive because latent and intermittent defects escape manufacturing test; a Synopsys-TSMC demo showed monitor/test/repair over UCIe using high-speed interfaces (PCIe/UCIe), signal-integrity monitors, spare-lane repair, and physical-aware bump testing that cut bridge counts 95-99%.

insights.claim: A device that passes at time zero is no longer a guarantee of reliability over its lifetime, making in-system testing essential to detect emerging defects from silicon aging, environmental conditions, or stress in mission-critical hyperscale data center and automotive systems.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Reliability/SLM shifts from a one-time manufacturing gate to a lifecycle service, expanding the addressable market for DFT and monitoring IP vendors (Siemens, Synopsys, proteanTecs).
insights.quote: Having a device that works at time zero is no longer a guarantee of reliability over its lifetime.
insights.locator: Key Takeaways / Having a device that works

insights.claim: The AI revolution is outpacing the IC industry's ability to sufficiently test multi-chip systems for all necessary failure mechanisms at probe, final test, and system-level test, creating a widening test-coverage gap.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: A structural coverage gap driven by AI complexity is a secular tailwind for test-content, ATE time, and in-system test IP; watch test intensity per die rising.
insights.quote: The AI revolution is significantly outpacing the IC industry’s ability to sufficiently test multi-chip systems for all necessary failure mechanisms at probe, final test, and system-level test.
insights.locator: The AI revolution is significantly outpacing

insights.claim: Wider adoption of 2.5D systems and the push toward full 3D-ICs depends on in-system testing and the ability to monitor, detect, and repair failures in the field, especially where a chiplet or interconnect failure can be catastrophic.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: In-field repair capability is a gating dependency for advanced packaging adoption; treat monitor/test/repair maturity as a leading indicator for 3D-IC ramp.
insights.locator: The wider adoption of 2.5D systems

insights.claim: Silent data errors and silent data corruption are appearing in data centers from extensive long-duration IC use such as AI training, because manufacturing test does not stress transistors enough, letting latent small delay-type defects escape and fail in the field.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: SDC/SDE is a named, growing failure class in hyperscale AI fleets, driving demand for in-system test controllers and continuous reliability testing.
insights.quote: We’re seeing silent data errors, silent data corruption because of the extensive use of those ICs over a long period of time, for example, doing AI training.
insights.locator: In-system test is needed to catch what manufacturing testing cannot

insights.claim: Intermittent defects surface in the field because in-service power, voltage, and temperature conditions are very difficult to replicate in manufacturing test.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Field-condition-dependent defects justify moving test into the operating system, expanding the role of on-chip monitors over external ATE.
insights.locator: Secondly, intermittent defects show up

insights.claim: In-system testing must run not only during operation but also during power-up and power-down transitions, moments often overlooked that can expose vulnerabilities not detected under standard runtime conditions.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Transition-time testing is an under-served coverage niche; DFT tools that address it can differentiate.
insights.quote: These moments are often overlooked but can expose vulnerabilities that are not detected during standard runtime conditions,
insights.locator: It’s also important that in-system testing takes place

themes.theme: Self-repair via spare lanes and redundancy

insights.claim: Recent interface and bus standards including UCIe, AIB, and HBM4 provide spare lanes or pins so that when silicon-lifecycle-management data indicates a failure, swaps can be performed automatically to bypass the problem.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Spare-lane redundancy is being baked into mainstream chiplet/memory standards, making in-field self-repair a standard feature rather than exotic; benefits SLM/DFT IP attach rates.
insights.quote: Many recent standards for interfaces and buses, including UCIe, AIB (Advanced Interface Bus), and HBM4, provide spare lanes or pins.
insights.locator: Some chips can repair themselves in the field

insights.claim: The latest UCIe standard specifies two redundant bits for every 32 bits, enabling data lanes to shift when faults are identified.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Concrete ~6% lane redundancy overhead in UCIe quantifies the die-area cost of built-in repair; a spec-level detail for modeling chiplet interconnect design.
insights.locator: The latest standard specifies two redundant bits

insights.claim: Chips use dual modular redundancy by duplicating cores or triple modular redundancy with three registers, where a failing flop is outvoted by the other two.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Established redundancy schemes are being repurposed for lifecycle resilience, adding silicon overhead that favors larger, more expensive dies.
insights.locator: Some chips also duplicate cores for dual modular redundancy

insights.claim: Repair must occur not only in-field but also after assembly of multi-chip packages, pre-repairing known die-to-die defects via a fuse box, OTP, or non-volatile memory before shipping to users.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Post-assembly repair salvages expensive multi-die packages, improving package-level yield economics for advanced-packaging foundries.
insights.quote: when we send the package to our users, it is already repaired for the known defects that are there already in the package between the dies
insights.locator: We have to start doing it for manufacturing

insights.claim: In-field memory repair can be done as on-chip incremental soft or hard repair when a memory BiST controller fails, provided repair resources exist and the OTP can be programmed by raising voltages.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Demonstrates practical in-field repair mechanics that require pre-provisioned redundancy, reinforcing DFT/BiST IP demand.
insights.locator: Let’s assume that you are running this testing in the field

insights.claim: The root of the test challenge is the ever-widening ratio between silicon capacity and available pin count, because off-chip I/O has not kept pace with shrinking geometries, making slow GPIO test access impractical for large SoCs.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: The capacity-to-pin gap is the structural driver pushing test onto functional high-speed interfaces; a durable thesis for SerDes-based test IP.
insights.quote: The root of the test challenge is the ever-widening ratio between silicon capacity and available pin count,
insights.locator: This is where USB and PCIe shine

insights.claim: Using SerDes interfaces such as PCIe, USB, or UCIe for test instead of GPIO streaming scan (100-400 MHz) delivers a huge bandwidth gain that reduces test times by 10X to 20X.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: 10-20X test-time reduction directly cuts ATE cost per device, a quantified value driver for adopting SerDes-based DFT and reducing test capex.
insights.quote: if you use the SerDes, like the DFT, PCIe, USB or UCIe, there is a huge gain in bandwidth, which reduces test times by 10X to 20X
insights.locator: If you compare the bandwidth of GPIO

insights.claim: PCIe, originally a 2005 general-purpose expansion bus, has become the universal high-speed interconnect for GPUs, SSDs, AI accelerators, and CPU-to-GPU links and is now repurposed as the communications backbone connecting test hardware to the device under test.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Functional-pin reuse for test means PCIe/USB PHY IP gains a second value stream in SLM; reinforces the strategic value of interface IP portfolios.
insights.locator: In recent years, one key technology development

insights.claim: For AI accelerators, PCIe testing is critical because any defect can prevent the accelerator from being recognized by the host PCIe controller, cutting it off from the host CPU and system memory.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: PCIe integrity is a single point of failure for AI accelerator usability, elevating PHY test rigor as accelerator volumes scale.
insights.locator: For AI accelerators, PCIe testing is particularly important

insights.claim: Only high-speed testing with adequate power supplies and a cooling platform can confirm a PCIe PHY communicates at its generation's speed; standard scan, ATPG, and BiST only verify the interface was manufactured properly.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: At-speed PHY validation requires specialized power/thermal test platforms, a differentiated equipment and methodology niche.
insights.locator: While these tests ensure the interface was manufactured properly

insights.claim: Synopsys and TSMC built a two-die chiplet system on a silicon interposer in a 3NP process connected via a UCIe bus and demonstrated integrated monitor, test, and repair capabilities.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: A concrete Synopsys-TSMC reference flow on leading-edge 3NP signals commercialization of in-system multi-die test; a proof point for the ecosystem partnership.
insights.locator: A recent project between Synopsys and TSMC

insights.claim: The demo used IEEE 1838, whose parallel port (IJTAG) lets chips communicate with one another without requiring a PHY interface, and performed memory BiST test and repair through it.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: IEEE 1838 provides a PHY-less die-to-die test access path, an interoperability standard worth tracking for multi-vendor chiplet ecosystems.
insights.locator: Synopsys and TSMC demonstrated the health monitoring

insights.claim: Accessing a hidden die in the middle of a stack for test, repair, and monitoring becomes harder as die count grows, impacting manufacturing quality, yield, in-field reliability, RAS, and system performance.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Hidden-die accessibility is a key scaling bottleneck for 3D stacking, gating how many dies can be economically integrated.
insights.locator: The more dies, the more difficult the access

insights.claim: ATPG patterns for a hidden die route from PCIe through die 1, into its UCIe controller, across the UCIe bridge to die 2's UCIe controller, into decode IP, then into the target logic block.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Illustrates the multi-hop test-access architecture that IP vendors must supply for stacked dies, expanding controller and decode IP content per package.
insights.locator: Synopsys showed the path that ATPG pattern loading follows

insights.claim: Health monitoring of the UCIe link during mission mode is not part of the main UCIe standard and must be added as an add-on capability, even as UCIe PHY speeds move fast from 16 to 32 to 64 Gbps.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: A gap in the UCIe standard creates a differentiated IP opportunity for vendors selling mission-mode monitoring add-ons ahead of standardization.
insights.quote: One of the challenges is health monitoring during mission mode, which is not part of the main standards. Therefore, it has to come as an add-on capability.
insights.locator: Zorian described one challenge

insights.claim: Signal integrity monitors (SIMs) on the receiving PHY sample the UCIe signal to produce eye-diagram openings and report the percentage of degradation over time to a control engine, using the slower side band while the main band carries functional data.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: SIM-based degradation tracking is the sensing layer enabling predictive lane repair, a recurring IP content stream on every UCIe link.
insights.locator: To monitor the health of signals

insights.claim: A physical-aware die-to-die test uses the bump map file to check whether a bridge between C4 bumps physically makes sense, reducing the number of bridges by 95% to 99% and cutting bridge test patterns by half.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: A 95-99% bridge reduction and 2X pattern cut materially lowers stack-level test cost, and it is being written into the IEEE standard, favoring adopters.
insights.quote: This reduced the number of bridges by 95% to 99%. And, by the way, it reduced the number of test patterns that you would generate for the bridges by half.
insights.locator: So we built a physical-aware die-to-die test

insights.claim: Direct microbump testing was rejected because 20µm bumps on 25µm pitch are too small to probe; instead probing was done on multiple 50x50µm sacrificial pads with optical inspection and continuity testing of the 65nm interposer.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Microbump probing limits force sacrificial-pad workarounds, adding die-area and process steps; a concrete constraint for probe-card and interposer design.
insights.locator: The decision was made not to perform direct microbump testing

themes.theme: On-chip monitors, ML analytics, and power-aware test

insights.claim: proteanTecs deploys ML models trained on deep data from on-chip agents across multiple post-silicon stages to predict deviations for each individual die regardless of its position in the distribution, enabling personalized quality and power/performance management.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Per-die predictive analytics is proteanTecs' differentiated moat; watch attach rates as chiplet designs demand die-level lifecycle data.
insights.locator: Enabling effective feed-forward and feed-back

insights.claim: Time-to-failure modeling in data centers correlates degradation trends with usage profiles to enable predictive maintenance, and predictive analytics are already delivering tangible results across the lifecycle.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Predictive maintenance is moving from concept to deployed value in data centers, supporting recurring-revenue analytics models tied to fleet uptime.
insights.quote: Predictive analytics are already delivering tangible results across the lifecycle.
insights.locator: On-chip monitors and analytics can predict impending failures

insights.claim: Chiplet-based designs need to shift-left with smart testing and outlier detection at wafer sort to avoid finding a defective chiplet only after costly assembly, while die-to-die interconnect can only be tested after assembly where ATE has no external access.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Known-good-die economics push test spend earlier (wafer sort) and onto on-chip agents for post-assembly interconnect, reshaping where test value accrues.
insights.quote: You need to be able to do smart testing and outlier detection at wafer sort to avoid detecting a defective chiplet device after assembly due to its cost.
insights.locator: On-chip monitors, or Agents, also play a critical role in outlier detection

insights.claim: proteanTecs on-chip agents monitor per-lane signal timing in mission mode to identify marginal lanes as candidates for lane repair on the ATE and in-field.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Per-lane marginality detection feeds the spare-lane repair loop, tying monitoring IP directly to actionable in-field repair.
insights.locator: By leveraging our on-chip agents

insights.claim: Power-aware ATPG is gaining ground because power and heat are increasingly problematic during test, minimizing switching activity during scan shift and holding scan cells in fixed states during capture to significantly lower both dynamic and peak power.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Test-power management is emerging as a required capability for dense 3D-IC test, a differentiator for ATPG tool vendors like Siemens EDA.
insights.locator: Because power and heat are becoming so problematic during test

insights.claim: Embedded deterministic test (EDT) patterns applied during in-system test enable efficient targeted detection of latent defects from silicon aging, voltage fluctuations, thermal stress, or workload-induced degradation.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Reuse of manufacturing EDT patterns in the field lowers the barrier to continuous reliability testing, leveraging existing DFT investment.
insights.locator: Using embedded deterministic test (EDT) patterns
